danger_signals.visual_form: Player lifting feet before starting signal on mat
danger_signals.meaning: Rule violation; in tournament mode, three false starts result in disqualification

danger_signals.visual_form: White box markers appearing along screen edge during 110M hurdles event
danger_signals.meaning: Required jump point; failure to jump at correct time results in collision/performance penalty

safety_signals.visual_form: Player character in starting block position on mat before 'on your mark' signal
safety_signals.meaning: Safe to begin; no false start penalty if feet remain stationary

safety_signals.visual_form: Track stadium environment with defined running lanes visible on screen
safety_signals.meaning: Safe running space; organized competition environment

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Computer-controlled opponent athletes displayed on opposite side of track/field
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Left side of Family Fun Fitness mat used for dash and hurdle events; either side for jump events

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Six distinct computer-controlled athletes with different visual representations (tournament opponents swapped species across regional versions)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Displayed racing against or competing simultaneously with player character on same track

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stadium background colors and configuration change; opponent athlete species/appearance differs by region (Japanese vs. US/European variants); text layout adjusted for regional copyright
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Computer players display competitive racing behavior; false start rule enforcement activated; progression requires beating each of six opponents sequentially at dash and hurdle events

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Track stadium environment displays with multiple player slots registered (up to six alternating players per event); Guinness 1982 world track record reference displayed for scoring context
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All four events available consecutively; scoring measured against real-world athletic benchmarks rather than relative competition

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Regional Version (Japan vs. US vs. Europe)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sky color shifts (dark blue in Running Stadium vs. light blue in Stadium Events); national flags change (Japanese to US); stadium background colors recolored; logo styling differs; copyright text positioning and length affects layout; 'Mat B' graphic rebranded to 'Power Pad' in re-release
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tournament opponent character swaps species; UI text localization; sound effect variations ('on your mark' signal differs in US re-release and Europe); Bandai branding removed in World Class Track Meet version

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 100M Dash: side-by-side racing lanes. 110M Hurdles: identical to dash with white box jump markers overlaid. Long Jump: single runway with sand pit visualization. Triple Jump: runway with three distinct jump marker zones.
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mat input method varies: left side required for dash/hurdles; either side valid for jumps. Top speed controlled by which mat row player activates. Jump duration directly affects distance scoring in jump events.

environmental_storytelling.example: Background stadium structure (bleachers, field markings, sky) remains consistent across all four event types, establishing cohesive athletic venue identity and legitimacy

Flag swaps and color palette shifts communicate market-specific branding and cultural localization without changing core gameplay; sky color darkness suggests mood/era differences between Japanese and Western releases
Visible side-by-side running lanes in dash/hurdle events establish fair competition format and spatial separation between player and opponent
White box markers for hurdles appear progressively along screen edge, providing visual pace and rhythm cues for timing-dependent jumps

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Any foot lift before 'on your mark' signal triggers false start penalty; three violations in tournament mode cause disqualification
Speed determined by which row of mat buttons activated; sustained input maintains velocity; hurdle jumps required at white box marker locations
Running velocity builds on approach; jump initiation freezes character in air; duration airborne determines distance score; triple jump requires three sequential jump markers
Each of six sequential opponents displays competitive pacing; attempts to outrun/out-jump player; visual appearance varies by regional version
False start detection active; disqualification on third violation; strict enforcement creates tension and consequence for timing errors

notes: Stadium Events is a fitness-focused exergame with minimal narrative world-state complexity. Visual communication is primarily functional—establishing athletic event types, competition framework, and rule enforcement through UI, environmental consistency, and character positioning. Regional variants demonstrate how aesthetic/cultural markers (flags, sky color, logo style, opponent character design) communicate market identity without altering core mechanics. The game communicates world-state primarily through event selection UI and opponent presence rather than dynamic environmental changes. Danger/safety signals are primarily mechanic-based (false starts, hurdle timing) rather than narrative-based.
